content.text: Matter is composed of extremely small particles. To understand this, consider the process of breaking down chalk: A stick of chalk can be broken into two pieces, then further into smaller pieces, and then ground into fine powder. When observed through a magnifying glass, the fine powder reveals tiny grains, each still composed of chalk. If this grinding were to continue indefinitely, eventually a point would be reached where the chalk pieces cannot be broken down further into smaller units. These fundamental building blocks are called constituent particles.

Definition: A constituent particle is the basic unit that makes up a larger piece of a substance or material. When a substance cannot be broken down further, the resulting tiny units are its constituent particles.

content.title: Breaking Down Chalk (Activity 7.1)
steps: Break a chalk stick into two pieces
Continue breaking into progressively smaller pieces
Grind the small pieces using mortar and pestle
Observe the fine powder under a magnifying glass
Notice each speck is still chalk
Understand that further grinding would eventually reach constituent particles

content.text: Grinding chalk is a physical change (not chemical) because chalk does not transform into a new substance—only its particle size decreases. The same logic applies to sand, clay, and other materials: the visible grains and particles we observe are not the smallest units but are themselves composed of countless constituent particles.

When sugar dissolves in water, the sugar particles break down into constituent particles so small they cannot be observed, yet their presence can be sensed through taste. The sweetness throughout the water indicates the dissolved sugar particles have spread through the liquid. These tiny particles occupy spaces between water particles called interparticle spaces—regions between particles where no matter exists.

content.title: Sugar Dissolution in Water (Activity 7.2)
steps: Fill a glass tumbler with drinking water
Add two teaspoons of sugar without stirring
Taste water from the top layer—no sweetness detected
Stir until sugar completely dissolves
Taste water again—sweetness detected throughout
Observe no visible sugar particles, yet they exist as constituent particles

content.text: The dissolved sugar particles are invisible because they are the constituent particles of sugar, not observable grains. These particles occupy available spaces between water molecules. Both chalk and sugar demonstrate that matter is composed of a large number of extremely small particles, so minute they cannot be seen even through an ordinary microscope.

notes.section: 7.2 What Decides Different States of Matter?

topics.topic: Interparticle Forces and State of Matter

content.text: Constituent particles of matter are held together by attractive forces called interparticle attractions. The strength of these attractive forces depends on two factors: (1) The nature of the substance—different substances have different interparticle forces, and (2) The interparticle distance—even slight increases in distance drastically decrease the attractive forces. The strength of these interparticle forces fundamentally determines the physical state of a substance (solid, liquid, or gas).
Key principle: Stronger interparticle attractions = particles held closely together = solid state. Weaker interparticle attractions = particles farther apart and freer to move = liquid or gas state.

Acharya Kanad, an ancient Indian philosopher, first proposed that matter is composed of tiny, indivisible, eternal particles called Parmanu (atoms). This concept, recorded in his work Vaisheshika Sutras, predated modern atomic theory by centuries and represents an early understanding of matter's particulate nature.

Solids have a definite shape and volume because constituent particles are tightly packed with very strong interparticle attractions. These strong forces hold particles in fixed positions, preventing free movement. The solid state is characterized by: (1) Definite shape—cannot be changed without external force, (2) Definite volume—remains constant, (3) Particles in fixed positions—held by strong interparticle attractions, (4) Limited particle motion—only vibration around fixed positions, no movement past each other.

content.title: Properties of Solids (Activity 7.3)
steps: Collect various solid objects (iron nail, rock salt, stone, wood, key, aluminum)
Observe fixed shapes and sizes
Attempt to hammer them
Notice resistance to shape change
Conclude particles are strongly held together in fixed positions

content.text: When a solid is heated, its constituent particles vibrate more vigorously. At a critical temperature called the melting point, these vibrations become so vigorous that particles can overcome the interparticle attractive forces and begin leaving their fixed positions. The solid transforms into a liquid. The melting point is the minimum temperature at which a solid melts into a liquid at atmospheric pressure.

Different solids have different melting points due to different strengths of interparticle attractions. Weak interparticle forces = low melting point. Strong interparticle forces = high melting point.

Liquids have a definite volume but no fixed shape—they take the shape of their container. This is because interparticle attractions in liquids are weaker than in solids, allowing particles freedom to move but only within limited space. Liquid state characteristics: (1) No fixed shape—adopts container shape, (2) Definite volume—remains constant, (3) Particles can move—free movement within limited space, (4) Weaker interparticle attractions—than solids but still substantial.

content.title: Liquid Properties and Shape (Activity 7.4)
steps: Take three containers of different shapes (A, B, C)
Mark 200 mL level in each container
Fill container A with water to marked level
Transfer water to container B—water takes new shape but volume stays 200 mL
Transfer to container C—water takes that shape, volume remains 200 mL
Conclusion: Liquids have definite volume but adopt container shape

content.text: Evidence that interparticle forces in liquids are weaker than solids: When moving a finger through water, you can push it without breaking the liquid continuously. The liquid temporarily yields to the finger's movement, then returns to its original position when the finger is removed. This demonstrates that while liquid particles attract each other, these forces are not strong enough to maintain fixed positions.

When a liquid is heated, particle movement becomes increasingly vigorous. At the boiling point, particles have enough thermal energy to escape the liquid entirely, transforming into vapor (gas). The boiling point is the temperature at which a liquid boils and transforms into vapor at atmospheric pressure. At boiling point, vapor forms rapidly throughout the liquid (bubble formation). Below boiling point, evaporation occurs—slower vapor formation at only the surface.

Gases have no fixed shape and no fixed volume—they expand to fill all available space and take the shape of their container. This occurs because interparticle attractions are negligible (nearly zero), allowing particles complete freedom of movement in all directions. Gas state characteristics: (1) No fixed shape—fills container completely, (2) No fixed volume—expands to fill available space, (3) Particles move freely—in all directions with high speeds, (4) Negligible interparticle attractions—particles act nearly independently.

content.title: Gas Expansion and Space Filling (Activity 7.5)
steps: Create smoke by burning an incense stick
Place gas jar A upside-down over smoke to trap it
Cover jar A with glass plate
Place gas jar B upside-down on glass plate (covering A)
Remove glass plate carefully
Observe smoke spreading into jar B to fill entire space
Conclusion: Gases spread to occupy all available space

content.text: The smoke in the experiment represents gas particles. The tiny smoke particles suspended in air are constantly hit by invisible gas particles, and this motion helps observe gas particle behavior. Smoke spreading throughout both jars demonstrates that gas particles move freely and expand to fill available space. Iodine vapor can also demonstrate this principle—when solid iodine is placed in a closed jar, the purple vapor spreads throughout, showing gas expansion.

Fluids: Both liquids and gases flow and do not retain fixed shape. These properties classify them as fluids, distinguishing them from solids. Fluidity is the key characteristic shared by liquids and gases.

notes.section: 7.3 How Does Interparticle Spacing Differ in Three States?

content.text: The spaces between particles (interparticle spacing) differ significantly across the three states of matter. Compressibility—the ability to reduce volume by applying pressure—reveals differences in interparticle spacing. Gases are highly compressible because particles have large spaces between them. Liquids are essentially incompressible because particles are much closer together. Solids are incompressible because particles are in close, fixed positions.

content.title: Gas Compressibility (Activity 7.6)
steps: Take a syringe without needle
Pull plunger fully outward
Place thumb over open end
Push plunger slowly and steadily inward
Observe volume of air decreases as plunger moves in
Remove pressure—air particles spread, plunger returns to original position
Conclusion: Gases compress easily due to large interparticle spacing
Repeat with water—water is practically incompressible

content.text: The syringe experiment reveals: (1) Air has large spaces between particles—easily reduced by pressure, (2) When pressure is removed, gas particles spread again and plunger returns, (3) Water shows no volume reduction with pressure—particles already closely packed, (4) This demonstrates particle spacing is largest in gases, smallest in solids, intermediate in liquids.

content.title: Interparticle Spaces in Liquids (Activity 7.7)
steps: Fill glass vessel half-full with water and mark level A
Add two teaspoons of sugar and mark new level B
Stir to dissolve sugar
Mark final water level as C
Observe: Initial level rises to B (sugar adds volume), then decreases to C (lower than B)
Conclusion: Solution volume < water volume + sugar volume, indicating interparticle spaces

content.text: The volume decrease after dissolution occurs because dissolved sugar particles occupy spaces already present between water particles (interparticle spaces). The total volume doesn't equal the sum of component volumes because the dissolved particles fit into existing gaps. This experiment demonstrates that despite close packing, interparticle spaces exist in liquids (though smaller than gases). Sand, being insoluble, settles and increases total volume because sand particles cannot occupy pre-existing water spaces.

Why sugar dissolves but sand doesn't: Sugar particles break down to constituent particle size and occupy interparticle spaces in water. Sand particles are still large aggregates and settle on the bottom, creating a mixture where total volume increases.

Magnified schematic representations show: Solids have particles tightly packed with minimal interparticle spacing, but some space exists (though empty/vacuum). Liquids have slightly more space between particles than solids, allowing particles some movement freedom while maintaining close contact. Gases have maximum interparticle spacing with particles far apart, allowing complete freedom of movement in all directions.

Important clarification: Interparticle spaces contain nothing (vacuum/empty space), not air. The spaces are empty regions between particles, not air trapped between them. This distinction is important for understanding matter at the particulate level.

The relationship between state and spacing: Solid < Liquid < Gas (in terms of interparticle spacing). This progression directly relates to particle freedom of movement: fixed position (solid) → limited movement (liquid) → free movement (gas). The state of matter is fundamentally determined by the balance between interparticle attractive forces and thermal (heat) energy of particles.

notes.section: 7.4 How Particles Move in Different States of Matter?

topics.topic: Particle Movement and Diffusion in Liquids

content.text: Particles in matter are in constant motion. In liquids, this motion can be observed through diffusion—the gradual spreading of one substance through another. When potassium permanganate (a deep pink solid) is placed in water, initial observation shows pink streaks spreading from the crystal. Over time, the entire volume of water becomes uniformly pink, indicating the potassium permanganate particles have spread throughout via diffusion.

content.title: Diffusion in Water (Activity 7.8)
steps: Place a few grains of potassium permanganate in a glass of water
Observe initial pink streaks spreading from the crystal
Watch as pink color gradually fills the entire glass
Notice uniform pink color after time—all water uniformly colored
Conclusion: Water particles are in constant motion and spread permanganate particles throughout

content.text: The spreading occurs because water particles, in constant random motion, hit potassium permanganate particles and pull them apart (if soluble) or spread them around (if insoluble like sand). The particle movement increases with temperature: In hot water, permanganate spreads fastest; in room-temperature water, slower; in cold water, slowest. This temperature effect on diffusion rate demonstrates that particle movement increases when heat is provided.

Solubility connection: Substances like sand remain insoluble because water particles cannot overcome the strong interparticle attractions holding sand grains together. Sugar dissolves because its constituent particles are small enough and the interparticle attractions weak enough for water particles to separate and disperse them.

topics.topic: Particle Movement in Gases—Diffusion and Spreading

content.text: Gas particles move freely and rapidly in all directions. When an incense stick is lit in one corner of a room, its fragrance is initially detected only nearby. Within minutes, the fragrance is perceived throughout the room. This occurs because fragrant gas particles spread through the air, carried by constantly moving air particles that hit and help disperse fragrance molecules in all directions.

content.title: Diffusion of Gas Fragrance (Activity 7.9)
steps: Light an incense stick in one corner of a room
Initially, fragrance detected only near the stick
After a few minutes, fragrance noticeable throughout the room
Eventually, uniform fragrance distribution in entire room
Conclusion: Gas particles move freely and spread throughout available space

content.text: Real-life examples of gas particle movement: Cooking smells spreading throughout a house (cooking vapors and aromas diffusing through air), perfume spray spreading (fragrant gas particles diffusing), bleach fumes detected from a distance (chlorine gas particles diffusing), exhaust from vehicles spreading in streets (combustion gases diffusing in air). All demonstrate that gas particles move constantly and spread throughout available space.

Understanding particle behavior explains everyday cleaning: When soap is applied to oil-stained fabric, numerous soap particles surround the oil particles. Soap molecules are unique: one end attaches to oil (hydrophobic), the other mixes with water (hydrophilic). This arrangement allows soap to lift oil from fabric and wash it away with water. The effectiveness of soap cleaning is based on the particulate nature of matter—soap particles work at the molecular level to interact with both oil and water particles.

topics.topic: Role of Thermal Energy in State Transitions

content.text: The physical state of matter is fundamentally determined by thermal (heat) energy of particles relative to interparticle attractive forces. In solids: Low thermal energy causes particles to remain close together, experiencing strong interparticle attractions, motion restricted to vibration. At melting point: Thermal energy increases sufficiently to overcome attractive forces, particles break free from fixed positions, solid converts to liquid. In liquids: Moderate thermal energy allows particles freedom within limited space, interparticle spacing increases slightly. At boiling point: Thermal energy is sufficient for particles to escape entirely, liquid converts to gas. In gases: High thermal energy ensures particles move freely in all directions, interparticle spacing maximum, negligible attractive forces.

Energy-state relationship: Thermal energy determines state of matter. Different substances have different melting and boiling points because they have different interparticle attractive forces. Strong forces require more thermal energy to overcome, resulting in higher melting/boiling points.

glossary.definition: Anything that has mass and occupies space. Matter can exist in three physical states: solid, liquid, or gas.
The fundamental building blocks that make up matter. When a substance cannot be broken down further, the resulting tiny units are its constituent particles (atoms and molecules).
The attractive force that holds constituent particles together. The strength depends on the nature of the substance and the distance between particles.
The empty space (vacuum) between constituent particles in matter. These spaces exist in all states but are smallest in solids and largest in gases.
A state of matter with a definite shape and volume. Particles are tightly packed with strong interparticle attractions and are unable to move freely from their fixed positions.
A state of matter with a definite volume but no fixed shape. It takes the shape of its container. Particles can move freely but only within limited space.
A state of matter with no definite shape or volume. It expands to fill the entire container. Particles are free to move in all directions with negligible interparticle attractions.
The minimum temperature at which a solid melts to become a liquid at atmospheric pressure. Different substances have different melting points.
The temperature at which a liquid boils and transforms into vapor (gas) at atmospheric pressure. Different substances have different boiling points.
The process where liquid particles escape into the gaseous state at temperatures below the boiling point. Occurs slowly and only at the liquid surface.
The ability of a substance to reduce its volume when pressure is applied. Gases are highly compressible, liquids are essentially incompressible, solids are incompressible.
The gradual spreading and mixing of particles of one substance with another, driven by the constant motion of particles. Observable in liquids and gases.
A substance that flows and can change shape. Both liquids and gases are classified as fluids because they share the property of fluidity.
The energy possessed by particles due to their motion. Higher temperature means higher thermal energy and faster particle movement.
A change in which the identity of the substance remains the same but its physical properties (like size or shape) change. Breaking or grinding chalk is a physical change.
The ability of a substance to dissolve in another substance. Sugar is soluble in water (dissolves to constituent particles), while sand is insoluble (remains as particles).
Rapid back-and-forth movement of particles around fixed positions. In solids, particles vibrate but cannot move past each other.
The process of a solid transforming into a liquid when heated to its melting point. Particles overcome interparticle attractions and begin moving freely.
The process of a liquid transforming into a gas (vapor) when heated to its boiling point. Particles escape entirely from the liquid phase.
The smallest unit of a chemical element that retains the properties of that element. Multiple atoms combine to form molecules.
A group of atoms bonded together, forming the smallest unit of a substance. Molecules are the constituent particles of most matter.
An ancient Indian concept (by Acharya Kanad) referring to tiny, indivisible, eternal particles of matter. An early precursor to modern atomic theory.

summary_points: Matter is composed of extremely small constituent particles that cannot be seen even through an ordinary microscope. All substances—whether visible grains like sand or dissolved substances like sugar—are made of countless minute particles held together by interparticle attractive forces.

Interparticle attractions are the attractive forces holding constituent particles together. The strength of these forces depends on the nature of the substance and the distance between particles. Strong interparticle attractions result in tight particle packing; weak attractions allow particles freedom of movement.

The physical state of matter (solid, liquid, or gas) is fundamentally determined by the strength of interparticle attractions relative to particle thermal energy. Solids have the strongest interparticle attractions, liquids have weaker attractions, and gases have negligible attractions.

Solids have definite shape and volume because particles are tightly packed with very strong interparticle attractions, held in fixed positions where they can only vibrate. The tightly packed arrangement and strong forces make solids rigid and incompressible.

Liquids have definite volume but no fixed shape because particles have weaker interparticle attractions than solids, allowing freedom to move within limited space. Liquids take the shape of their container while maintaining constant volume, making them fluids alongside gases.

Gases have no definite shape or volume because interparticle attractions are negligible, allowing particles to move freely in all directions and expand to fill available space. Gases are highly compressible and diffuse readily to mix with other gases.

Interparticle spacing progressively increases from solids to liquids to gases. Solids have minimal spacing but some exists; liquids have moderate spacing allowing particle movement; gases have maximum spacing allowing complete freedom. Despite close packing in solids and liquids, interparticle spaces (empty regions) exist in all states.

When a solid is heated to its melting point, particles gain enough thermal energy to overcome interparticle attractions and transition to liquid state. When a liquid is heated to its boiling point, particles gain sufficient thermal energy to escape entirely into the gaseous state.

Particle movement is constantly occurring in all states of matter, observable through diffusion. In liquids, this motion spreads dissolved substances throughout; in gases, fragrant particles spread to fill entire spaces. Temperature increases particle movement speed: hotter = faster movement, colder = slower movement.

The particulate nature of matter explains practical phenomena including diffusion of liquids, spreading of gases, compressibility differences between states, dissolution of soluble substances, and even how soap cleans oil-stained fabric by working at the particle level.
